items.mistake: Adding more training data to fix high training AND validation error
items.fix: Increase model capacity, add relevant features, or reduce excessive regularization — then re-check the learning curve.
items.why: High bias is structural misspecification; extra rows shrink variance but not squared bias.
items.example: Linear model on quadratic data: 10k vs 100k points both leave similar train MSE; polynomial features fix the gap.

items.mistake: Confusing high bias with label noise or data quality issues
items.fix: Check whether a more flexible model on the same clean labels improves train error; if yes, the bottleneck is model bias, not noise.
items.why: Irreducible error and bias both raise error, but remedies differ — noise needs better labels or features; bias needs richer hypotheses.
items.example: Train MSE stuck at 2.0 with depth-1 tree but drops to 0.3 with depth-8 on identical (X, y) — bias was the limiter.

items.mistake: Using 'bias' to mean fairness or demographic skew in ML interviews
items.fix: Disambiguate: estimation bias in bias–variance vs social bias in fairness topics; answer using the decomposition when asked about underfitting.
items.why: Mixing terms causes wrong diagnoses — fairness mitigation does not fix underfitting from a linear model on nonlinear data.
items.example: Interviewer asks about high bias in a model: explain E[ŷ] − f*(x), not disparate impact across groups unless they specify fairness.

items.mistake: Maxing model complexity without checking whether train error is already low
items.fix: If training error is already high, address bias first (capacity, features) before regularizing for variance.
items.why: Regularization and simpler models increase bias — harmful when you are already underfitting.
items.example: Strong L2 on a linear model with train accuracy 60% on a separable problem — loosen regularization or use a nonlinear classifier first.
